coredata.dc:title: Green production of tunable multicolor nanoparticles with diamond structure from long-flame coal by a one-step mild oxidation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Chong, Junkai
Cheng, Xiang
Xiao, Longheng
Guo, Min
Gao, Jianming
Cheng, Fangqin
Zhang, Mei
Huo, Xiangtao
coredata.dc:description: The complex compositions and structural features of low-rank coal obstruct its wide use in materials applications directly. Graphene nanocarbon hydrosol with uniform particle size and containing nanodiamond structures were prepared from long-flame coal by a simple H2O2 oxidation, achieving the highest extraction rate of 30.86%, and the depolymerization degree was 80.21% when the temperature was 80 °C. The average diameter of the carbon nanoparticles was 1.86 ± 0.62 nm with 2–3 graphite flake layers, and these graphite flake layers were derived from the stable crystalline domains in the raw coal. Carbon nanoparticles exhibited good excitation wavelength-dependent and concentration-dependent fluorescence, which can be tunable from blue to green fluorescence by adjusting the concentration (0.19–1.5 g/L) and excitation wavelength (340–460 nm), the reason may be due to the combined effect of the relative intensity changes of various types of oxygenous groups, low-lying effects resulting from n(OH)→π*(CO) interactions between the hydroxyl groups and carbonyl groups, and the aggregation of particle. The post-reaction residue has a similar structural composition to that of humic acid and could be used for developing functional materials and agricultural restoration. Therefore, preparing carbon nanoparticles and humic acid by mild oxidation of hydrogen peroxide for the high-value utilization of long-flame coal has good application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long-flame coal
Oxidation stripping
Carbon nanoparticles
Concentration-dependent fluorescence
Nanodiamonds

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of the reaction temperature on the extraction efficiency and depolymerization degree of long flame coal.

Morphological and structural characterizations of the synthesized nanoparticles from coal. (a) A low-magnification image of the as-synthesized nanoparticles and their size distributions (the inset). (b, c, d, e) High-resolution TEM micrographs of the nanoparticles. (f) EDS spectrum of the nanoparticles.

Structural analysis of carbon nanoparticles: (a) XRD spectra; (b) Raman spectroscopy; (c) Raman fitting in the 800-1800 cm−1 wavelength range; (d) Raman fitting in the 2200-3200 cm−1 wavelength range.
Schematic diagram of the reaction between H2O2 and coal.
Fluorescence emission spectra of samples at different concentrations (The inset showed the digital photograph of the sample under 365 nm light).
(a) Stokes shift versus excitation wavelength; (b) UV–visible spectra and (c) FT-IR spectra of 0.19 g/L samples.
Emission spectra of different concentrations of carbon nanoparticle hydrosol at specific excitation.
CIE 1931 chromaticity chart of different concentrations of carbon nanoparticles.
Solid-state 13C NMR spectra and fitting curves.
FT-IR spectra of oxidized residues samples.
Proximate and ultimate analyses of long-flame coal samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: M, moisture; A, ash; V, volatile matter; FC, fixed carbon; ad, air-dried base; St, daf, total sulfur in dry ash-free.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absolute quantum yields of carbon nanoparticles at different concentrations.
Chemical shift values and molar percent of different carbon types.

head.ce:title: Green production of tunable multicolor nanoparticles with diamond structure from long-flame coal by a one-step mild oxidation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Metallurgy
School of Metallurgical and Ecological Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The complex compositions and structural features of low-rank coal obstruct its wide use in materials applications directly. Graphene nanocarbon hydrosol with uniform particle size and containing nanodiamond structures were prepared from long-flame coal by a simple H2O2 oxidation, achieving the highest extraction rate of 30.86%, and the depolymerization degree was 80.21% when the temperature was 80 °C. The average diameter of the carbon nanoparticles was 1.86 ± 0.62 nm with 2–3 graphite flake layers, and these graphite flake layers were derived from the stable crystalline domains in the raw coal. Carbon nanoparticles exhibited good excitation wavelength-dependent and concentration-dependent fluorescence, which can be tunable from blue to green fluorescence by adjusting the concentration (0.19–1.5 g/L) and excitation wavelength (340–460 nm), the reason may be due to the combined effect of the relative intensity changes of various types of oxygenous groups, low-lying effects resulting from n(OH)→π*(CO) interactions between the hydroxyl groups and carbonyl groups, and the aggregation of particle. The post-reaction residue has a similar structural composition to that of humic acid and could be used for developing functional materials and agricultural restoration. Therefore, preparing carbon nanoparticles and humic acid by mild oxidation of hydrogen peroxide for the high-value utilization of long-flame coal has good application prospects.

ce:section.ce:para: Coal occupies a dominant position in China's energy consumption, and its clean and efficient utilization is the foundation to ensure energy security and solve existing environmental problems [1,2]. Low-rank coal accounts for about 55% of China's total coal resources, are more abundant and generally less expensive than high-rank coals such as anthracite, this makes them more accessible for large-scale applications and commercialization, but in the confines of current technology, the coal mainly used to burn for electricity [3]. However, the high moisture content and low calorific value of low-rank coal will cause environmental pollution during the combustion process [4,5]. Due to their higher volatile matter content and porosity, low-rank coals can be more reactive than high-rank coals. This enhanced reactivity can be beneficial for certain thermochemical processes, and also, low-rank coals often contain a higher number of functional groups, which could be advantageous for chemical modifications or derivatization. Therefore, utilizing the activity of the lower rank coals and treating them by controlled oxidation methods, converting coal into various clean and high-value-added fuels, chemicals, and carbon materials et al. is the most reasonable way to achieve low-rank coal's clean and efficient utilization [6].

The structure of low-rank coal is significantly different from other regular coals, it has been reported in the literature that low-rank coal not only contains a large number of aliphatic structures, small aromatic ring structures, and sizeable oxygen-containing heteroatom functional groups, but also has characteristics of macromolecular network structure, the high density of intramolecular covalent bonds leads to the poor extraction performance [7,8]. Coal liquefaction has mild conditions and better development prospects than coking and gasification. Traditional liquefaction methods such as thermal dissolution polymerization have harsh reaction conditions and complex processes, increasing the cost of liquefaction. On the contrary, the liquefaction oxidation method is a simple process with mild reaction conditions, and it's also an effective way to study the coal structure, and meanwhile it is an essential means of stripping coal pairs to obtain chemicals, especially oxygenated organic compounds [9]. Compared with other oxidation methods, hydrogen peroxide is easy to obtain, economical and environmentally friendly, more importantly, its mild reaction conditions allow highly selective oxidation of low-rank coals, achieving high yields of small-molecule fatty acids. Miura oxidized lignite with 30% H2O2 at 60 °C for 24 h, and found that 71% of the carbon in the coal was converted to water-soluble acids, which were rich in methanol, formic acid, acetic acid, glycolic acid, and succinic acid [10]. Xiong studied the effect of reaction time on the conversion of low-rank Naomaohu coal and oxidation products, the results showed that H2O2 could break bridge bonds and side chains connecting to aromatic clusters, and the hydration products were mainly monocyclic compounds [4]. In addition to sufficient depolymerization to generate small molecule compounds, the preparation of carbon nanoparticles by controlling the degree of oxidation has also attracted attention. Hu adopted the ultrasonic method using H2O2 to treat anthracite coal and successfully prepared the carbon dots, which could quickly degrade organic dyes under visible light [11]. Tonkeswar processed Indian low-rank coal using the ultrasound-assisted wet chemical method to prepare nanodiamonds, which exhibited good fluorescence properties [12]. Zhang synthesized blue-green emitting carbon dots under high pressure using hydrothermal reactions of coal degradation liquefaction products, exhibiting significant laser-dependent fluorescence emission [13]. In previous studies, most researchers usually devoted efforts to oxidizing coal samples thoroughly for obtaining chemical products, preparing carbon nanoparticles or assessing the carbon nanoparticles' fluorescence performance. Nevertheless, the preparation methods are often ultrasonic and high pressure, requiring special and complex equipment, thus the use of simple and mild oxidation methods to treat low-rank coals, achieving its full component utilization was often neglected.

In this paper, based on the previous research on the structure of Shanxi long-flame coal (a typical low rank coal) [8], the non-polluting hydrogen peroxide condensing reflux method was used for oxidizing the Shanxi low-rank coal, the effect of different oxidation temperatures on coal conversion was investigated, and the fluorescence performance of carbon nanoparticles at different concentrations was studied, the structural composition of the post-reaction residue was assessed. It was the first time that the fluorescent tunable of the carbon nanoparticles were realized based on low-rank coal by a straightforward method. The work reported here provides new insights into the high-value utilization of low-rank coal and understanding of the fluorescence mechanism of nanoparticles, and provides a new approach for full component utilization of low-rank coal.

The washed long-flame coals were obtained from the northern Shanxi province in China, dried at 80 °C for 24 h, then the coal sample was crushed to below 200 mesh. Industrial analysis of the proximate and ultimate analyses of the sample were given in Table 1 . H2O2 (30%; Sinopharm) was used to exfoliate and oxidize the coal. Fresh 0.45-μm polytetrafluoroethylene membranes (Jinlong, China) were used for collecting the purifying products.

1 g long-flame coal sample was placed in a round-bottom flask and 200 mL of 20 wt% H2O2 solution was dropped, magnetically stirred at a set temperature (40, 60, 80,100, 120, 140 °C) and subjected to condensation reflux for 9 h. After the condensing and refluxing, the reactant was filtered through a 0.45-μm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane to collect the hydrosol, the residue was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h and weighed. Measuring the carbon concentration and volume of the nanocarbon hydrosol, the extraction efficiency and depolymerization degree were calculated using the following formula [14].
(1)
Extraction
efficiency
( % )
=
(
C 1
V 1
)
/
m 1
× 100 %
(2)
Depolymerization
degree
( % )
=
( 1 −
m 2
/
m 1
)
× 100 %
Where,
C 1
is the concentration of the collected hydrosol, g/L; V1 is the hydrosol volume, L; and
m 1
is the coal quality before the reaction, g;
m 2
is the residue quality after the reaction, g.

The collected hydrosol after the oxidation reaction were diluted 2, 4 and 8 times, their concentrations were measured and corresponding fluorescence properties were characterized. The carbon nanoparticles were freeze-dried from the collecting hydrosol for the necessary structural characterization.

The structure and morphology of the materials were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, America). X-ray diffraction (Rigaku, Japan), Raman spectroscopy (Bruker SENTERRA, Germany), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet IS 10, American), UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, Xipu TU-1901, China), were used to study the functional groups and other structural information of carbon nanoparticles. Fluorescence spectroscopy (F7000, Hitachi, Japan) was used to test the fluorescence properties of carbon nanoparticles and the quantum yield was measured by fluorescence spectrometer (FLS920, Edinburgh, United Kingdom). The carbon concentration in the hydrosol was detected by Total organic carbon analyzer (TOC, Shimadzu TOC-L CPH, Japan). Residues were examined using solid-state NMR (Bruker Avance Ⅲ 400 M, Germany).

The long-flame coal was oxidized at different temperatures (40–140 °C), the extraction efficiency and depolymerization degree of the reaction were investigated. Fig. 1 showed the carbon extraction efficiency and depolymerization degree at different temperatures, from which it could be seen that the depolymerization degree tends to increase with the increase of the reaction temperature first and then remains stable. The carbon depolymerization degree was 80.21% when the temperature was 80 °C, and remained stable at 96% when the temperature continued to increase. Meanwhile, it also could be seen from Fig. 1 that the extraction efficiency tends to increase and then decrease with the increase of oxidation temperature, when the temperature was 80 °C, the extraction efficiency reached up to 30.86%.

Hydrogen peroxide contains hydroxyl radicals, which were considered to be the strongest oxidizing substances. When the oxidation temperature was insufficient, the amount of OH− decomposed by hydrogen peroxide was limited, and it was difficult to break the bonds and oxidize the coal. But when heated, hydrogen peroxide could selectively break weak bonds in coal, oxidizing organic carbon chains to CO2 and H2O [11], the escape of carbon dioxide resulted in a reduction of the carbon content in the hydrosol. The increase in depolymerization degree showed that the carbon in the coal reacts sufficiently with hydrogen peroxide, resulting in the coal being fully depolymerized, and the oxidation efficiency was close to 100%, indicating that the coal was fully depolymerized after heating at 140 °C.

When the temperature was 80 °C, the coal depolymerization degree was 80.21%, and the extraction efficiency was highest at 30.86%. Hence, the subsequent experiment was carried out under this condition.

The long-flame coal was oxidized at 80 °C and the nanoparticle hydrosol was diluted to 0.19 g/L, which was obtained by diluting the original hydrosol 8 times for the next step of the study. Fig. 2 (ãe) showed the TEM images of the nanoparticles, indicating that the nanoparticles in hydrosol were mainly distributed from 1 to 5 nm in diameter and they were dispersed, uniformly sized and crystalline, as confirmed by the Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT) pattern (inset Fig. 2c,d,e); in particular, the nanoparticles had an average diameter of 1.86 ± 0.62 nm, as shown in the inset in Fig. 2a (The number of carbon nanoparticles exceeds 300 in the statistics, obtained from a Gaussian fit by the imagine J software, R-squared 99.69%).

For carbon nanoparticles, the structure was mostly single crystal structure with a lattice spacing of 0.210 nm and 0.340 nm, respectively, which was close to the (100) and (002) diffraction facets of graphite carbon, and about 8∼10 stacked monoatomic graphite layers as shown in Fig. 2b, therefore, carbon nanoparticles could be defined as graphite carbon dots.

It is interesting to note that when the particle size was larger than the average size, many carbon nanoparticles were found to be more than a simple single crystal structure (Fig. 2d ∼ e), it also tends to exhibit twinning (as shown in Fig. 2d), the nominal sizes of the carbon nanocrystals were found to be 10 nm (Figs. 2d), 7.5 nm (Fig. 2e), respectively. The planner spacing of the crystal lattice fingers was measured to be in the range of 2.1 Å∼2.3 Å (Fig. 2d−e), which was in good agreement with the lattice planes of various diamond phases, including the cubic diamond (111) (2.06 Å), lonsdaleite (002) (2.06 Å) and (100) (2.18 Å) as reported elsewhere [12,15]. These measurements indicate that the carbon nanocrystals were considered not only graphene quantum dots, but also contained nanodiamonds.

The chemical composition was assessed by energy-dispersive spectroscopy as depicted in Fig. 2f, indicating that the particles mainly consist of carbon, trace Si was also observed, from impurities within the coal sample, and Cu elements arise from the copper TEM grid. The energy spectrum also indicates O elements in this nanoparticle, which may result from the oxidation groups adsorbed on the surface.

Fig. 3 a, b demonstrated the XRD and Raman spectra of the carbon nanoparticle hydrosol after freeze-drying. The XRD patterns were shown in Fig. 3a, which appeared to be a broad band centered at around 26°, corresponded with the (002) planes of carbon, suggesting a long-range disordered characteristic of the nanoparticles, it also could be seen that weak shoulder peaks at 41.31°, corresponding with the (100) planes of carbon, which also supported that the as-prepared nanoparticles mainly consist of disordered carbon atoms [16,17]. Hence, similar to other reported literature, the nanoparticles consisted of graphene carbon dots and nanodiamonds [12,18], which was in agreement with the TEM results in Fig. 2.

Raman spectroscopy is a non-destructive method employed to detect the carbon crystal structures, assess their quality, and determine their composition. This technique relies on the scattering of light by the vibrational modes of Raman-active molecules. When applied to carbon nanoparticles, Raman spectroscopy serves a more extensive purpose than merely identifying the diamond and graphene phase; it also yields valuable insights into their structural characteristics, composition, and surface functionalities. To further study the structural characteristic of the nanoparticles, Raman measurement on the nanoparticles sample was performed with 532 nm laser excitation. The Raman spectra (Fig. 3b) showed two main characteristic bands appearing at 1574 and 1343 cm−1, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 1343 cm−1 was known to be the lattice defect including the sp3 hybridized carbon, representing the disorder degree of carbon materials, these signals could overlap with the Raman peak of nanodiamonds [15]. Particularly, the G-band (∼1574 cm−1) was attributed to the vibration of the sp2 carbon atoms in the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice, depending on the graphitization extents of carbonaceous materials. In order to have a clearer description of the nanodiamond structure in carbon nanoparticles, the main characteristic peaks in the wavelength range of 800∼1800 cm−1 were matched, as shown in Fig. 3c. Raman spectrum was deconvoluted into four distinct bands at 1235 cm−1, 1330 cm−1, 1428 cm−1 (D peak) and 1574 cm−1 (G peak). The peak at 1235 cm−1 was produced by the coherence scattering of the smallest nanodiamond grains [19]. The spectral characteristics of nanodiamonds showed a typical Raman profile of nanodiamonds, with a significant band at 1330 cm−1 ascribed to the nanodiamond lattice, indicating the production of nanodiamond-like carbon, which was also evident from TEM micrographs [20]. The peaks at 1428 cm−1 (D peak) and 1574 cm−1 (G peak) were attributed to the disordered sp2 carbon present in the sample and first-order scattering of the E2g vibration of graphene respectively. The typical peaks labeled at 2700 cm−1 correspond to 2D bands. They represented a few layers of graphene structure, which further proved the graphene structures of the coal nanoparticle [21]. The 2D band of nanoparticles was much broader than that of graphene sheets, as shown in Fig. 3d, very similar to the observations in the previous publications [22], this particular broadening in the 2D band could be due to the relaxation of the double-resonance Raman selection rules, associated with the random orientation of graphene nanoparticles with respect to each other [23]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3d, the 2D peaks can be divided into 4–6 Lorentz peaks, this may be due to the splitting of the electron energy band structure of the multilayer graphene, indicating that the number of basic graphene structure layers in carbon nanoparticles was 2∼3 layers [24], after agglomeration it exhibited a structure such as 8 to 10 layers, as shown in Fig. 2b and c.

In our previous study, the structure of this long-flame coal has been investigated, and demonstrated that coal was a complex organic matter consisting of fatty and aromatic structures [8]. The aromatic layer diameter of this coal was 1.89 nm and the average number of aromatic flakes per stack was 3.78 flakes, the coal has a relatively open structure. Combined with the analysis of TEM images (inset of Fig. 2a), the average diameter of nanoparticles was 1.86 ± 0.62 nm, which was approximately equal to the size of graphite lamellae in this coal (1.89 nm). The graphene structure layers in carbon nanoparticles were 2∼3 layers as evidenced by Raman spectroscopy, which was approximately the same as the 3.78-layer graphite structure of coal, suggesting that the carbon nanoparticles may come directly from the stable crystalline carbon in coal, herein the organic carbon chains were destroyed during the oxidative exfoliation process, while the crystalline carbon retained due to its strong intermolecular forces.

Fig. 4 showed the schematic diagram of the reaction between coal and H2O2. According to the literature, hydroxyl radicals generated by hydrogen peroxide preferentially react with the weak bonds in coal, the fatty structure attached to the graphite-like structure in coal was preferentially destroyed [11], leaving a stable crystalline carbon (Fig. 2b ∼ e), the graphite-like structure was retained to produce the so-called nanodiamonds and graphene particles.

Tunable fluorescence under different excitations was an important property of carbon nanoparticles. The concentration of the original hydrosol after the reaction was measured to be 1.5 g/L and the concentrations after dilution 2, 4 and 8 times were 0.75, 0.38 and 0.19 g/L. Fig. 5 showed the fluorescence spectra of the filtrates with different concentrations of carbon nanoparticles. For the same concentration, it was observed that the maximum emission wavelength exhibits a red-shift trend with increasing excitation wavelength, which was similar to the reports on the fluorescence of nanodiamonds [12]. The inset in Fig. 5 was the digital photograph of the carbon nanoparticles hydrosol at different concentrations under UV light (at 365 nm), it could be seen that the four different concentrations of nanoparticles hydrosol exhibited different fluorescence intensities, it showed the trend from dark to bright as the concentration decreases.

The Stokes shift, defined as the difference in the positions of the maxim band between excitation and emission spectra, has been widely used to confirm the quantum confinement effect in nanocrystals. If the fluorescence mechanism was affected by the size distribution, the Stokes shift trends linearly to zero with increasing excitation, indicating the existence of the band gap of the bulk material [25]. However, in our case, the fluorescence of the carbon nanoparticles did not meet this criterion. Instead, the Stokes shift exhibited nonlinearity, decreasing first and remaining stable with increasing excitation wavelength, an example of a 0.19 g/L sample was shown in Fig. 6 a. These phenomena thus suggested that the fluorescence of carbon nanoparticles was driven by mechanisms other than the size-induced quantum-confinement effect [12]. The occurrence of the red shift and excitation dependence of the fluorescence was possibly ascribed to the combined effect of the relative intensity changes of various types of oxygenous groups and low-lying effects resulting from n(OH) → π*(CO) interactions between the hydroxyl groups and carbonyl groups, and it was also found in other literature [25].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the nanoparticles measured showed a very significant terminal absorption (Fig. 6b), from which a strong absorption band centered in the deep UV region (233 nm) can be seen, attributing to the intrinsic absorption of nanodiamonds (
σ −
σ ̽
transition), which matched with the energy gap of diamond (5.3 eV) [20], a small shoulder peak at around 225 nm was in connection with the π−π* transition of the C=C bonds [26]. The bands that appeared at around 250−350 nm were due to the excitation of π-electrons (π→π*) of the aromatic π system, while a shoulder at 300 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition of C=O bonds. The broad absorption with a gradual to long wavelength proved the existence of band tails due to defect states [12]. As a result, the prepared nanoparticles contain a large number of unsaturated chromophore structures such as aromatic rings and nanodiamonds particles [16,27].

In order to understand the influence of various surface functional groups on the observed fluorescence properties of carbon nanoparticles, FT-IR spectroscopy was performed on the carbon nanoparticle filtrates, the results were shown in Fig. 6c. The FT-IR spectra of the carbon nanoparticles showed stretching vibrations of O–H (hydroxy) at 3410 cm−1, C=C (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons) skeletal vibration peaks at 1450 cm−1, the vibrational absorption band of C = O at 1635 cm−1, stretching vibration of C–O in C–O–C (epoxy) groups at 1391−1076 cm−1, and vibrational absorption band of C–H at 1535 cm−1 [26,28]. As can be seen from Fig. 6c, the peak intensity of the O–H group was significantly higher than that of the C=O group, therefore, the emission showed blue fluorescence, as reported elsewhere [25].

Particularly, under the same excitation wavelength in Fig. 7 , the emission wavelength exhibited a red-shift trend with increasing concentration, showing a strong concentration-dependent fluorescence trend.

By controlling the concentration of carbon nanoparticles and the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence could be tunable from blue to green. Normalizing the fluorescence emission spectra from Fig. 7 showed that the maximum emission wavelength changes from 440 nm to 510 nm with the concentration of nanoparticles increased from 0.19 to 1.50 g/L. This may be due to when the concentration was low, the distance between the particles being too large, and electrostatic repulsive force leading to difficulty in aggregation. As the concentration of nanoparticles increases, the distance between the particles becomes shorter, and noncovalent bonding was dominant, such as hydrogen bonds, thus promoting aggregation [16]. The concentration-dependent red-shift was similar to the size effect of the graphene carbon quantum dots, where the increase in size caused a shorter band gap, resulting in fluorescence emission red-shift [29].

Fig. 8 showed the CIE chromatograms for different concentration conditions. It could be seen that the maximum emission wavelength shifted from blue to green with the concentration of the hydrosol increased.

In addition, the absolute quantum yield test showed that the quantum yields decreased with increasing concentration, as shown in Table 2 , which was the same trend as reported in the literature [16]. Compared to the quantum yields of carbon dots prepared from high-quality carbon sources (0.38–1.1% for anthracite [30] and 2.1% for graphite [31]), we achieved a quantum yield of 2.8% when the concentration of carbon nanoparticles was 0.19 g/L using only low-rank coal.

In addition to the carbon nanoparticles generated by the oxidation of coal, the remaining components were called oxidation residues. In order to further understand the structure and composition of oxidized residue, the residue after reaction at 80 °C was characterized by 13C NMR analysis, the results were showed in Fig. 8. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 8 that there were two main peaks at 0∼95 and 95∼165 ppm, representing aliphatic carbon and aromatic carbon, respectively. In addition, another indistinct peak could be seen at 165∼200 ppm, which was about the carbonyl carbon. These three peaks could be further divided into 13 individual peaks via curve fitting, the fitted data were calculated, and the information of the corresponding splitting peaks was shown in Table 3 , the peak separation was consistent with the literature reports published [8,32].

It could also be seen from Fig. 9 and Table 3 that the area of aromatic carbon attributable to 90∼165 ppm was slightly larger than others, indicating that aromatic carbon was the main component in the oxidized residue, the relative content of aromatic carbon at 90∼165 ppm was 52.90%, the proportion of aliphatic carbon was 39.19% and the proportion of carbonyl carbon was 7.91%.

In comparison of the proportion of each structure in the long-flame coal before the reaction (aliphatic carbon: 23.73%, aromatic carbon: 75.31%, carbonyl carbon 0.96%) [8], the aromatic structure was significantly reduced in the residue, whereas the fatty structure and carboxyl structure were increased greatly. It showed that during the coal oxidation and depolymerization, the aromatic lamellae were stripped off and the residue was fully oxidized, demonstrating again that aromatic structures dominated the main structure in carbon nanoparticles.

After the oxidation of coal by hydrogen peroxide, the residue was rich in oxygen-containing functional groups and had enhanced reactivity [33,34], Fig. 10 showed the FT-IR spectrum of the oxidized residue, which exhibited a structural composition similar to that of natural humic acid [34,35], The oxidized residues showed a wide absorption peak between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 was related to the –OH stretching vibration of carboxylic (–COOH) groups and absorbed water molecules. In addition, other functional groups and chemical bonds present in the residue have been similarly identified, and their consistency with those reported in the literature has been confirmed [26,36,37].

Carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on oxidized residue serve as active sites for a range of chemical reactions, examples include their ability to participate in esterification and acylation, this facilitates the functional tailoring of the carbon material [38]. The capacity of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups to form complexes with metal ions augments their application spectrum to metal ion sequestration, catalytic processes, and the synthesis of metal nanoparticles anchored to the carbon base [39]. Additionally, these groups impart hydrophilicity to carbon structures, thereby enhancing their dispersibility in aqueous systems. And the electrochemical dynamics of carbon substrates are also amenable to modifications in the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, influencing their efficacy in electrochemical devices like batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells [40], which could be used in fields such as functional materials or agricultural restoration.

In summary, by adjusting the oxidation temperature, H2O2 was used to oxidize Shanxi long-flame coal, and achieved the highest extraction rate of 30.86%, and the depolymerization degree was 80.21% when the temperature was 80 °C. At this time, graphene nanocarbon hydrosol with uniform particle size and containing nanodiamond structure were prepared, and the average diameter of carbon nanoparticles was 1.86 ± 0.62 nm, with 2∼3 graphite flakes, and these graphite flake layers originated from the stable crystal domains in the raw coal. The carbon nanoparticles exhibited good excitation wavelength-dependent and concentration-dependent fluorescence, and the maximum emission wavelength could be tuned from 440 nm to 510 nm by adjusting the concentration between 0.19–1.5 g/L and excitation wavelength between 340–460 nm, presenting blue to green, wavelength-dependent fluorescence may be due to the combined effect of the relative intensity changes of various types of oxygenous groups and low-lying effects resulting from n(OH) →π*(CO) interactions between the hydroxyl groups and carbonyl groups, and the concentration-dependent fluorescence may be due to the agglomeration of carbon nanoparticles. The residue has a similar structural composition to natural humic acid, which could be used as a functional material for further development. Our work has explored a novel preparation method, making the coal-based nanoparticles more diversified and meaningful, and also providing a new approach for full component utilization of low-rank coal.

Junkai Chong: Investigation, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Xiang Cheng: Investigation, Methodology. Longheng Xiao: Investigation, Methodology. Min Guo: Supervision, Validation. Jianming Gao: Supervision, Visualization. Fangqin Cheng: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Mei Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiangtao Huo: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U21A20321).
